pathophysiology_summary: Tinea capitis is a dermatophyte infection of the scalp/hair (often zoophilic Microsporum canis from a new puppy) producing the triad of patchy alopecia, scaling and 'black-dot' hairs broken flush with the scalp, with follicular pustules. A kerion is a brisk cell-mediated hypersensitivity reaction to the fungus — a boggy, exquisitely tender, suppurative inflammatory mass studded with follicular pustules, spongy throughout (not a single fluctuant pus cavity), with tender regional lymphadenopathy. Critically, a kerion is NOT a bacterial abscess: incision & drainage is inappropriate and harmful (pain, scarring) and does not treat the fungus. Discriminators: preceding patchy alopecia/scaling/black-dots and a spongy multi-pustular mass distinguish it from a unilocular staphylococcal abscess; broken hairs/scaling/painful mass distinguish it from seborrhoeic dermatitis; and the inflammation/pustules/scaling distinguish it from the smooth non-inflamed skin of alopecia areata. Treatment is SYSTEMIC oral antifungal (mandatory — topicals cannot penetrate the hair root; e.g., griseofulvin or terbinafine per paediatric guidance, for 6–8 weeks), a short oral corticosteroid to rapidly reduce the kerion's inflammation and prevent scarring alopecia, and adjunct antifungal shampoo to reduce shedding/contagion, plus contact/hygiene measures and checking the puppy. Do not squeeze/incise the mass. Safety-net for secondary bacterial cellulitis/systemic illness.

differential.evidence_basis: Patchy alopecia + scaling + black-dot hairs then a boggy suppurative mass; new puppy source.
A boggy pus-oozing mass could be an abscess — but the preceding tinea triad and spongy multi-pustular (non-fluctuant) mass indicate a kerion; misdiagnosis leads to harmful I&D.
Scaly scalp, but no patchy alopecia/broken hairs or a painful inflammatory mass.
Smooth non-inflamed hair loss, not inflamed scaly broken-hair patches with a kerion.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise a kerion (NOT an abscess — no I&D), start SYSTEMIC antifungal + short steroid, and give contagion/animal-source advice.

differential.evidence_basis: Spongy multi-pustular mass on a tinea-capitis background; no systemic sepsis.
Spongy, multi-pustular, non-fluctuant mass on a tinea background — not a drainable abscess.
Working diagnosis: systemic oral antifungal (mandatory) + short oral steroid for the kerion + antifungal shampoo + contact/animal measures; do NOT incise & drain.

red_flags.description: Kerion misdiagnosed as a bacterial abscess leading to inappropriate incision & drainage
red_flags.clinical_significance: I&D of a kerion is harmful (pain, scarring) and does not treat the fungus; the correct treatment is systemic antifungal + steroid.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unnecessary surgery, scarring alopecia, and untreated infection.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial cellulitis / systemic illness (spreading redness down the face/neck, high fever, lethargy)

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the kerion as a bacterial abscess and performing/advising I&D.
Prescribing only antifungal shampoo/cream — topicals cannot cure tinea capitis; systemic antifungal is mandatory.
Not using a short steroid to limit scarring, or not addressing contagion and the animal source.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation to confirm boggy (vs fluctuant) mass
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Start SYSTEMIC oral antifungal + a short oral steroid for the kerion + antifungal shampoo, arrange in-person confirmation/cultures and paediatric dosing, and give contagion/animal-source advice; ED if secondary cellulitis/systemic illness. Do NOT incise & drain.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Incising a kerion, or treating with topicals only.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Tinea capitis requires systemic antifungal therapy (griseofulvin/terbinafine per organism/age) for weeks; a kerion is managed with systemic antifungal ± a short corticosteroid and is not incised; treat contacts/animal source.
